summary: Gruber dissects Eric S. Raymond's essay about failing to configure a shared printer on Linux and his subsequent proposals for improving open source usability. Gruber argues that Raymond's framing — that open source just needs minor attitude adjustments to reach 'Aunt Tillie' — fundamentally misunderstands the depth of the problem. The real issue isn't that Linux is almost there; it's that even expert Linux users can't figure out the software, and UI design is an order of magnitude harder than Raymond acknowledges. Gruber contends that the open source model is structurally ill-suited to producing good end-user software because great UI requires sustained work by talented, paid professionals under strong creative direction. He points to the success of commercial software (Mac OS X, Windows) and developer-facing open source tools as evidence that the market has already sorted this out. The essay concludes that desktop Linux is cheap and fast but not good, and you generally get what you pay for.
key_points: Raymond's inability to configure his own printer undermines his framing of usability as an 'Aunt Tillie' problem — the real issue is that even Linux experts struggle with Linux desktop software
UI design is not a finishing touch or attitude adjustment; it's an order of magnitude more work than building the underlying functionality, requiring rare talent and sustained investment
The open source model works well for developer-level tools (Apache, Python, GCC) but structurally works against good end-user software, which requires strong unified direction like filmmaking

The business model matters: selling 'services and support' for software that's supposed to be so intuitive it needs no support is a contradiction, which explains why the best UIs come from commercial software
Unix nerds who care about usability are switching to Mac OS X, draining the pool of talented developers who might otherwise improve desktop Linux
money_quotes: UI development is the hard part. And it's not the last step, it's the first step.
If there's a glib, nutshell synopsis for why Linux desktop software tends to suck, it's this: Raymond and his ilk have no respect for anyone but themselves.
Great software developers don't design for morons. They design for smart, perceptive people — people just like themselves. They have profound respect for their users.
The idea that GUI software needs to be designed for 'dumb users' is completely wrong.
The open source revolution has done nothing to change the fact that the best-designed, most-intuitive user interfaces are found in closed-source commercial software.
Movies are collaborative art, but require strong direction. So it is with end user software.
It's pretty hard to sell 'services and support' for software that fits that bill. The model that actually works is selling the software itself.
There's an old engineering adage: 'Fast, good, cheap: pick two.' Desktop Linux software is cheap and fast, but it's not good.
key_insight: Good UI isn't a coat of paint you apply after building the real software — it's the hardest part of the work, requiring rare talent, strong direction, and sustained investment that the open source model is structurally unable to provide.
